summary: Cathy O'Neil argues that due to inadequate COVID-19 testing in the U.S., confirmed case counts are unreliable indicators of the pandemic's severity. She proposes using hospital bed utilization as a better proxy for tracking the outbreak's true scale. While acknowledging this metric has limitations—including lag time and confounding factors like postponed elective surgeries—she argues it's more reliable because hospitals track beds accurately and the data is less susceptible to political manipulation. She calls for journalists to collect and compare hospital bed data across national chains.
key_points: Confirmed COVID-19 case counts are unreliable due to severely inadequate testing rates in the U.S.
Hospital bed utilization provides a more politically-independent metric since hospitals reliably track this data
The death count is likely undercounted because many coronavirus deaths went untested
This proxy still underestimates the problem due to postponed elective surgeries and reduced transmission of other illnesses from social distancing
Data transparency websites should add 'tests administered' columns to contextualize their numbers
money_quotes: For terrible political and cultural and of course capitalistic reasons, we are not getting tested at reasonable rates.
We are not actually seeing enough to know how bad things really are.
Considering that we don't know who is infected, we should use as a proxy of our problem not the 'confirmed case' count but rather the 'hospital beds used above normal' count.
Counting beds is relatively free of political manipulation.
Our death count is most assuredly way too low because there have likely been plenty of people dying of coronavirus who just never got tested.
key_insight: When official metrics are corrupted by political and systemic failures, we should seek alternative proxies that are harder to manipulate and closer to ground truth.
